glossary.definition: A field of computer science that deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes like reasoning, discovering meaning, generalizing, or learning from experience.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience.

glossary.definition: Humans, animals and even plants possess intelligence. Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc. However, it is psychology that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence.
glossary.sentence: To figure out what artificial intelligence is, this concept should be considered in a close connection with our understanding of what is intelligence in general. In this topic, you will find out what we understand by intelligence...

glossary.definition: A field of science that has developed a set of tests allowing assessment of the level of human intellectual development. At the same time, this field of science has to some extent witnessed a crisis in the quest for what human intelligence is.
glossary.sentence: Some of the reasons are as follows. There is still no a unified viewpoint if intelligence is a single ability or a set of abilities... It is problematic to measure intelligence due to the diversity of people, which is determined by, for example, linguistic, cultural, physical condition and other differences.

glossary.definition: The question arises what kind of human intelligence should be adopted as a standard in the development of intelligent computer systems: average intelligence for humanity, the intelligence of some genius individual, or the intelligence of a child with autism spectrum disorder?
glossary.sentence: Many tests that are developed in psychology for assessing human intelligence are static and do not measure individual's ability to learn and adapt to his/her environment over time.

glossary.definition: It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. However it is even more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence, because animal diversity is even greater taking into account their cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs and the ways how they explore the environment in which they live.
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence... It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. However it is even more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence...

glossary.definition: It is currently known that plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system... At the same time, plants possess many forms of intellectual performance, such as they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time; they have a ‘sense of place’ and an awareness of the neighbourhood.
glossary.sentence: (Parise, Gagliano and Souza, 2020)... Thus, in order to cover the diversity of the existent manifestations of intelligent performance, a general definition of intelligence should encompass the essence of the different types of intelligence; not be limited to any particular set of senses, environments, goals, and any specific kind of intelligence implementation structures...

glossary.definition: A general definition of intelligence should: encompass the essence of the different types of intelligence; not be limited to any particular set of senses, environments, goals, and any specific kind of intelligence implementation structures; be based on principles which are fundamental and thus unlikely to alter over time; ideally be formally expressed, objective, and practically realisable as an effective intelligence test.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, the general definition of intelligence does not exist because we, people, still have sufficiently limited knowledge of the world in which we live...

glossary.definition: An approach that underlies systems that think humanly, considering the human being as an information processing system. It focuses on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system.

glossary.definition: An ideal intelligence standard, represented as logical rules in computer systems where knowledge is inferred through logical deductions.
glossary.sentence: The categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard

glossary.definition: A fundamental test in artificial intelligence to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It involves a tester asking questions via text-based input tool, and the AI must maintain successful communication, use knowledge representation for storage, automated reasoning for answers, and machine learning for adaptation.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly... To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have natural language processing, knowledge representation, automated reasoning, and machine learning capabilities.

glossary.definition: Entities capable of perceiving their environment and acting within it to achieve the best outcome or expected outcome in uncertain situations.
glossary.sentence: Systems acting rationally are based on the approach of rational agents... A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: A type of computing system loosely inspired by the structure and functioning of biological neurons. Despite having processing units with limited capability, when many are connected, they can perform complex tasks.
glossary.sentence: Although artificial neural networks have processing units with very limited computing capability, when many of these processing units are connected, the complete network is capable of performing complicated tasks (Callan, 2003).

glossary.definition: developing a mathematical model of data, using tools, frameworks, and libraries for building models automatically
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, there are many tools, frameworks, and libraries available for building machine learning models automatically. Using the existing tools and libraries, the developer of a machine learning model loads data and defines hyperparameters but rarely needs to write a machine learning algorithm from scratch.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that provided the initial idea of human mind as a physical device operating knowledge encoded in an internal language.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in philosophy...

glossary.definition: Provided mathematical formalization for computing, logics, and probability theory necessary for artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Mathematics provided tools to work with logical statements and with uncertain, probabilistic judgments. They also laid the foundations for reasoning algorithms.

glossary.definition: Offered theories on how to make choices leading to desired results and provided grounds for studies of rational agents in artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Economics offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.

glossary.definition: A scientific field related to artificial intelligence through its focus on information processing in humans and animals, reinforcing the idea that they can be seen as information-processing machines.
glossary.sentence: Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines. Their research questions are related to how humans and animals think and act.

glossary.definition: Provided artefacts that made possible development of artificial intelligence applications.
glossary.sentence: Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications.

glossary.definition: A constituent part of computer science that includes many chapters such as natural language processing, computer vision, machine learning, and robotics. The main goals are scientific (understanding principles of intelligent performance) and engineering (developing useful intelligent artefacts).
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence is a constituent part of computer science. It includes many chapters, such as natural language processing, computer vision, machine learning, robotics, etc.

glossary.definition: A theory that underlies practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems, and natural language processing. It suggests that intelligence arises from the manipulation of symbols according to formal rules (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: connectionist artificial intelligence

glossary.definition: A system that includes symbols representing meaningful patterns, symbolic structures connecting these symbols, operations on these structures, and the representation of operations themselves as symbols.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: A criticism proposed by John Searle suggesting that a physical system correctly manipulating physical symbols does not necessarily possess intelligence. It uses an example of a person in a room who, without understanding Chinese symbols, can produce correct outputs based on instructions.
glossary.sentence: Critics claim that the hypothesis is not enough to simulate human intelligence because intelligence is genetically biological and cannot be fixed by symbols (Luger, 2009). John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism.

glossary.term: distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units
glossary.definition: An approach to knowledge representation in connectionist AI where knowledge is not directly represented, but emerges from the interactions and numerical weights (connections) between artificial neurons (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: In connectionist artificial intelligence, there is an opposite view of the concept of knowledge: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: An AI approach focused on specific tasks such as chess playing or medical diagnosis. It is also known as immature or limited AI and has achieved significant progress so far (Pennachin & Goertzel, 2007).
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . This is the only approach in which significant progress has been achieved so far.

glossary.definition: An AI approach treating intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated. It aims to develop computer systems capable of solving wide-ranging tasks autonomously, performing them as well as humans (Russell & Norvig, 2020).
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: An AI approach focusing on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena across multiple spatial and temporal scales. It emphasizes the importance of grounding representations in the physical world to achieve true intelligence (Floreano & Mattiussi, 2008).
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008).

glossary.definition: An approach to AI where representations are based on real-world sensory input through sensors, allowing an intelligent system to perceive and interact with its environment (Brooks, 1990).
glossary.sentence: The main idea is that intelligence is a capability that arises when agents with simpler behaviours interact in the real environment and together achieve more complex behaviours (Copeland, 2018) .

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence which considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. Includes ant colony optimization, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization, inspired by birds and fish.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization, which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011).

glossary.definition: A sub-direction of swarm intelligence which studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization, which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011).

glossary.definition: A sub-direction of swarm intelligence which studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by bird and fish behaviour.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization, which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011).

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence techniques that do not rely on problem-specific knowledge but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: These ideas are represented in Figure 1. The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students. The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them. In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence techniques that rely on a large amount of problem-specific knowledge for problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence. Thus not all intelligent systems need the knowledge base because intelligent computer systems can also be developed using weak methods (symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations).

glossary.definition: A formal or mathematical representation used to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: How can we make this transition from the objects of the real world to the objects of intelligent computer systems? It leads to the idea that we need to have something between these two worlds. Do you have an idea what that might be? We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: A representation of the game's possible states and transitions between them, used in the minimax algorithm to determine game result and winning paths.
glossary.sentence: create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX

glossary.definition: Determining a current state (initial state), a desired future state (goal state), and the discrepancy between these two states to identify a problem.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states.

glossary.definition: The current state at the beginning of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: First, we determine the state that we are in at present (the initial state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: The desired state to reach during a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: Then, we decide on the state we would like to reach (the goal state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: A point in the state space graph representing a discrete state of the game. Each node displays states from which only one player can make a move.
glossary.sentence: One or more nodes are located at each level, and these display states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.

glossary.definition: A line connecting two nodes in the state space graph representing legal moves between discrete states of the game.
glossary.sentence: Arcs are used to represent legal moves in the game.

glossary.definition: A mathematical representation of a state space graph, consisting of nodes (N), arcs (A), initial state(s) (IS), and goal state(s) (GS).
glossary.sentence: From a mathematical point of view , a state space graph is represented by a four-tuple <N, A, IS, GS>, where N is the set of nodes. These correspond to states in a problem-solving process; A is the set of arcs between nodes. These correspond to steps/actions in a problem-solving process; IS is a nonempty subset of N. It contains root nodes or initial state(s) of a problem-solving process; GS is a nonempty subset of N. It contains leaf nodes or goal state(s) of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: In the state space graph, they represent initial states or states of affairs.
glossary.sentence: First, humans recognise their initial state or states of affairs which are represented by root nodes in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: In the state space graph, they represent desired states or states of affairs.
glossary.sentence: Second, humans define their desired state or states of affairs, which are displayed by leaf nodes of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: In the state space graph, they are represented by arcs and together with intermediate states define a course of problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: After that, humans determine resources and opportunities that together allow the identification of actions that can be undertaken to solve the problem. In the state space graph actions are represented by arcs, and together with intermediate states they define a course of problem-solving.

glossary.definition: A type of game where two players compete against each other, making alternate moves until the end. Both players have access to complete information about the course of the game and there are no randomness factors involved. One player’s loss is equal to another player’s gain, giving a total sum of zero.
glossary.sentence: To demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information .

glossary.definition: A characteristic of games where both players have access to complete information about the course of the game, including past moves, future moves, and available objects used in the game.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information . The following aspects characterise this type of game: two players participate in the game and compete against each other; both players have access to complete information about the course of the game (including past moves, future moves, and the available objects (e. g. cards or figures) used in the game).

glossary.definition: States that occur during a problem-solving process as a result of actions taken.
glossary.sentence: To eliminate the discrepancy between these states, we perform actions and pass through a series of intermediate states (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: The process of changing between game states by making legal moves in the game.
glossary.sentence: Playing a game can be seen as a problem-solving process. Let's take the game of tic-tac-toe as an example. In this game, the initial state of the problem-solving process is a blank grid, which appears when you start the game (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: A horizontal layer in the state space graph containing nodes representing states from which only one player can make a move.
glossary.sentence: The use of alternating moves in deterministic two-player games of perfect information limits us in terms of the creation of the state space graph. We must avoid using any arcs in the state space graph other than those between two adjacent levels, and especially arcs between nodes at the same level or arcs crossing several levels.

glossary.definition: A specific configuration of a game at any given moment in time, characterized by parameters related to the computer implementation of the game and its course. These parameters determine the values that change after making a move during the course of the game.
glossary.sentence: Each node level contains states from which only one of the two players can make a move. If an arc was drawn between two nodes at the same level, this would indicate that the same player could make two moves rather than only one, which would contradict the principle of a zero-sum deterministic two-player game of perfect information as defined at the beginning of this topic.

glossary.definition: A task of any search algorithm is to find a solution path if such a path exists in the state space graph (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: To be able to do that, the search algorithm needs to know a search start node and a search goal node, in addition to which, while searching for a solution, it should process a data structure – a state space graph (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: Ensuring each node in the state space graph represents a unique game state by avoiding multiple representations of identical states within a specific node level.
glossary.sentence: Nodes at the same level are identical if they have the same values for all parameters that characterise the states of the game. If there are identical nodes at the same level, they are not displayed several times, but only once per level, in order to save computational resources.

glossary.definition: Values that characterise a game state and determine its uniqueness. These can include the score of each player, the current game board, or both the scores of the players and the current game board.
glossary.sentence: The values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time; the values of the parameters should be represented in each node of the state space graph; the parameters depend on the game, and thus may be very different.

glossary.definition: The initial node in a search process from which the algorithm begins exploring the state space graph or tree.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A. The search goal node is K.

glossary.definition: A node that represents the target or objective in a search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is not the search goal node; thus, the algorithm starts the next iteration.

glossary.definition: A path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node. It corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem.
glossary.sentence: A solution path is a path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node. It corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem.

glossary.definition: A search direction where the search starts from root nodes and proceeds in forward direction in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: From the given data of a problem toward a goal. This is called data-driven search or forward chaining . The search process starts from a root node and it proceeds in forward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A search method suggested when (Luger, 2009) : A goal is given in the problem statement or it can easily be formulated. For example, if a state space graph represents a maze and finding an exit is necessary, starting from the exit would be wise as there are fewer paths leading from the exit to the entrance than vice versa.
glossary.sentence: Goal-driven search is suggested if (Luger, 2009) : A goal is given in the problem statement or it can easily be formulated. For example, if the state space graph represents a maze and it is necessary to find an exit, then it would be wise to start with the exit, because the number of routes leading from the exit to the entrance is smaller than the number of routes leading from the entrance to the exit (Figure 12).

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated, but are part of the current search process.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated, and thus are no longer part of the current search process.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: The manner in which the search algorithm navigates through the state space graph, either from data to goal (data-driven) or from goal to data (goal-driven).
glossary.sentence: In both cases, the search stops when the search goal node is reached or the state space graph is exhausted without finding the search goal node (Figure 7). In the context of the search direction, it should be considered that the location of the search start node and the search goal node depend on a search direction (Figure 8): If it is data-driven search, then the search start node is selected from the set of root nodes, but the search goal node– from the set of leaf nodes. If it is goal-driven search, then the search start node is selected from the set of leaf nodes, but the search goal node– from the set of root nodes.

glossary.definition: A characteristic used by the search algorithm to determine if a node matches the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: Using a measurable/comparable property of the search goal node. In this case, the search algorithm makes a simple comparison between the content of the current node and the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A solution path that minimizes a specified aspect (e.g., time, length, resource consumption).
glossary.sentence: Checking a property of the solution path, for example, by searching for the shortest path, the longest path, or a path visiting each node only once.

glossary.definition: A solution path that maximizes a specified aspect (e.g., time, length, resource consumption).
glossary.sentence: Checking a property of the solution path, for example, by searching for the shortest path, the longest path, or a path visiting each node only once.

glossary.definition: A method used in search algorithms that involves maintaining a list of nodes awaiting expansion, keeping track of nodes whose descendants/ancestors have not led to the search goal node, and storing information about the current path being tried. This allows avoiding cycles and considering the same node only once.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking introduces several practical aspects related to the implementation of other search algorithms (adopted from (Luger, 2009)) : a list of nodes awaiting expansion is needed to support backtracking; a list of nodes whose descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction) have not led to the search goal node should be kept; such a list will allow avoiding cycles and consideration the same node many times; it is necessary to keep information about the current path being tried; if the search goal node is found, then the solution path can be reconstructed.

glossary.definition: A node in a graph or tree structure that has no descendants and can only be an ancestor of other nodes.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible because it is a leaf node. Therefore, K is moved to the CLOSED list.

glossary.definition: A path in a state space graph where nodes connect and repeat, potentially causing a search algorithm to get stuck if not handled properly.
glossary.sentence: In this case, the search algorithm can get stuck in the cycle and never find the search goal node. Thus it would be logical to prevent the search algorithm from entering cycles, even though they are allowed in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A category of search algorithms that use only the state space graph or its nodes and arcs. They explore the state space sequentially without being able to determine whether one node is better than another based on reaching the search goal's cost.
glossary.sentence: In general, there are two main categories of search algorithms (Figure 1): Uninformed search algorithms use only the state space graph or, in other words, its nodes and arcs. This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: A search method that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
glossary.sentence: Depth-First Search is guaranteed to find the search goal node if it exists in the state space graph because this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the state space graph. However, this algorithm is not guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node, if such a path exists in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A list containing nodes yet to be expanded during the search process, ordered based on heuristic measures from best to worst.
glossary.sentence: The node K, which is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: A list containing nodes that have been expanded during the search process.
glossary.sentence: The node E, as an already expanded node, is moved to the CLOSED list.

glossary.definition: The node that the algorithm is currently examining or processing.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node. The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A node that can be reached by following a path from another node, called its ancestor, in a graph or tree structure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node F by generating its three descendants—nodes K, L, and G.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths, but may not find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit. It explores state space graph through a principle of deepening.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths. At the same time, it is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.

glossary.definition: A predefined maximum depth for Depth-Limited Search; once reached, the algorithm stops exploring further.
glossary.sentence: The second check gives a positive result, and the algorithm starts the next iteration. Iteration 3: The node J is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node. The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is not the search goal node; thus, the algorithm checks if its depth is lower than the depth limit. The depth level of the node J is equal to the depth limit.

glossary.definition: Nodes considered as leaf nodes (at the end of paths) or root nodes (beginning of paths), depending on the search direction, when the depth limit is reached in Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: A single step in a search process, during which nodes are expanded or evaluated based on specific criteria.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 3: The algorithm tries expanding the current node K. It is not possible because it is a leaf node. Therefore, K is moved to the CLOSED list.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores state space graph in a level-by-level fashion, using a 'First in, First Out' principle for OPEN list management. It moves onto the next level only when there are no more nodes to be explored at a given level.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion. This idea is represented in the title of the algorithm (Figure 14): “breadth” means to consider nodes sequentially at the same level before going deeper; “first” indicates finding the first path that leads to the search goal node and stopping the operation.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores all the neighbors at the present depth prior to moving on to nodes at the next depth level. It is guaranteed to find the shortest path if such a path exists in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search is also guaranteed to find the search goal node if this node exists in the state space graph, as this algorithm performs an exhaustive search of the space state graph. This algorithm is also guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node if such a path exists in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that searches for a goal node from both the start and goal perspectives simultaneously. It checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists to determine when to stop its operation. Bidirectional Search consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search and can find the shortest path to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search. Moreover, it is also able to find the shortest path to the search goal node.

glossary.definition: In Bidirectional Search, two OPEN lists are used - one for data-driven search and another for goal-driven search. These lists store nodes at the current depth level in each respective direction.
glossary.sentence: We will use two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists in this algorithm. Iteration 0: The search start node - the node A - is added to the OPEN list of data-driven search. The search goal node - the node K - is added to the OPEN list of goal-driven search.

glossary.definition: In Bidirectional Search, two CLOSED lists are used - one for data-driven search and another for goal-driven search. These lists store nodes that have been processed by the search algorithm in each respective direction.
glossary.sentence: Both CLOSED lists are empty because none of the nodes has been expanded yet.

glossary.definition: A common node found in both OPEN lists during Bidirectional Search, indicating that the search can stop as a path from the start to goal has been discovered.
glossary.sentence: In each iteration, after expanding the current nodes, it checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists. The algorithm stops if a common node is found or if the state space is fully searched, but a common node is not found.

glossary.definition: A type of search algorithm that uses heuristic knowledge, which are experience-based rules, to find a solution more effectively. Heuristics provide rules for choosing paths in a state space graph that are most likely to lead to an acceptable problem solution.
glossary.sentence: The effectiveness of finding the search goal node can be improved by using heuristic search algorithms.

glossary.definition: Problem-specific knowledge based on individual experiences used by humans to solve various problems in everyday or professional lives. In intelligent computer systems, it is represented as a mathematical function called the heuristic evaluation function.
glossary.sentence: The application of human heuristic knowledge could be fallible and may not give a solution, because every individual has different experiences, and rules based on heuristic knowledge are inherently individual

glossary.definition: A function that estimates the desirability of a particular state or node in a game tree based on certain criteria, often used in conjunction with minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning.
glossary.sentence: Both strategies described above use a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: Values assigned to leaf nodes in a state space graph based on who wins at a specific node. In the provided example, a value of 1 denotes a win by the maximiser, -1 represents a win by the minimiser, and 0 signifies a draw.
glossary.sentence: In this game, we have three possible results: A win by the first player. In the case considered here, it is the maximiser who starts the game, so we will use a value of 1 to denote a win by this player. A win by the second player. We will use a value of −1 to represent this state. A draw. We will use a value of 0 for this situation.

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and a given node n, allowing the algorithm to choose shorter paths available in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure characterizing the quality of a given node n or estimating the distance between the node n and the search goal node, allowing the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node

glossary.definition: A characteristic of states in problem-solving processes used to evaluate nodes in a state space graph indirectly by focusing on their importance for reaching the goal state.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): f(n) = ∑(ai * xi), where n – a node under consideration; ai – the weight of the ith factor; xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A factor used to evaluate nodes in a game of Tic-tac-toe, where any state with the computer's sign placed in corners is assigned a value of 3 (three potentially winning positions), and any state with the computer's sign placed in the center receives a value of 4 (four potentially winning positions).
glossary.sentence: To evaluate nodes in the graph, the system can apply a heuristic evaluation function based on such a factor as the number of potentially winning positions for the computer. The computer starts the game. It needs to select the most promising move. The system generates descendants of the initial state of the game and applies the defined heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.term: number of tiles out of place
glossary.definition: A factor used to evaluate nodes in the 8-puzzle, where the number of tiles not in their goal positions is calculated for each state when compared with the goal state.
glossary.sentence: We will consider only three states generated from the initial state of the game. The simplest factor to consider is the number of the tiles out of place in each state when it is compared with the goal state (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: A factor in heuristic evaluation functions that considers how many pieces are not in their intended places, with fewer tiles out of place indicating a closer proximity to the goal state.
glossary.sentence: The logic behind such a heuristic evaluation function is quite simple: the state that has the fewest tiles out of place is probably closer to the goal state. Applying the defined factor, the first and the third node will acquire a value of 5, while the second node will get a value of 3.

glossary.definition: A factor in heuristic evaluation functions that calculates the total distance of tiles from their intended places, with smaller sums indicating a closer proximity to the goal state.
glossary.sentence: Another factor that can be used to evaluate nodes is to sum up distances of tiles from their intended places (Figure 7). The logic is also very similar here: the smaller the sum of distances, the closer a state is to the search goal node. Applying the defined factor, the first and the third node will acquire a value of 6, while the second node will get a value of 4.

glossary.definition: A factor in heuristic evaluation functions that counts pairs of adjacent tiles needing to be swapped back and forth, with fewer reversals suggesting a closer proximity to the goal state.
glossary.sentence: We can define one more factor for this game: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8). This means that we need to change the places of two tiles located close one to another, and usually, it takes more than two moves to put them back. In our case, all three nodes will receive a value of 0, because there are no such situations on the board.

glossary.definition: The average number of descendants (children) each node in the state space graph has.
glossary.sentence: The interdependence of the branching factor (B), the depth (L), and the total number of nodes (T) is presented by the plot that you can see in Figure 11.

glossary.definition: The length of the longest path in the state space graph, usually referred to as L.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: The overall count of nodes in the state space graph, represented as T.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the total number of nodes (T) is...

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph until it reaches the goal node or finds a better node.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to a node in a game tree that represents an estimate of its quality or desirability.
glossary.sentence: it assigns a heuristic measure of −1, since in this state the minimiser has the higher score and wins the game

glossary.definition: Nodes that are directly generated or produced from a given node during an expansion process in the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E. None of the generated nodes have been added to the OPEN or CLOSED lists.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that incrementally expands the most promising node based on a heuristic evaluation function. It is guaranteed to find the search goal node if a path exists, but it can be computationally expensive.
glossary.sentence: Best-First Search is guaranteed to find the search goal node if a path between the search start node and the search goal node exists.

glossary.definition: A data structure used by Best-First Search to implement the OPEN list. It works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing algorithm where it does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node with a better heuristic measure than its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it. In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing algorithm where the search cannot continue if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it. In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: A compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search that selects a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration. This number is called beam width, and the algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider an algorithm providing a kind of compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search. Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: The number of best nodes examined during search in Beam Search, set before the algorithm starts searching.
glossary.sentence: This number is called a beam width . The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration.

glossary.term: Heuristic Search Algorithms for Game Development
glossary.definition: A type of algorithm used in game development, which includes methods like Best-First Search and the one mentioned in this text, aiming to imitate game playing and strive to win the game.
glossary.sentence: Topic 3.3 - Heuristic Search Algorithms for Game Development To implement a game, it is not sufficient to represent the game using a state space graph. We also need to equip an intelligent computer system with an algorithm that will process the graph, with the aim of imitating game playing and striving to win the game (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: A state space graph that includes all states from the initial state of the game to all goal states.
glossary.sentence: Definition A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: A more complex case of a state space graph where the results of the game cannot be evaluated at terminal nodes of sub-graphs.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: In the context of a complete state space graph, a value assigned to the root node that clearly shows the game's outcome if both players play rationally, without making mistakes, and with the aim of winning.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result . It clearly shows the result of the game if both players play rationally, without making mistakes, and with the aim of winning the game.

glossary.definition: In a complete state space graph, paths that start at the root node and reach a leaf node while satisfying certain conditions, showing the most likely course of the game if both players play rationally.
glossary.sentence: In the case of the complete state space graph, winning paths are identified based on the game result. These are not paths that definitely lead to a win by a particular player; they are paths showing the course of the game that is most likely to be undertaken.

glossary.definition: A decision-making algorithm used for minimizing the worst-case scenario in a game tree, often employed in two-player games such as chess or tic-tac-toe.
glossary.sentence: We have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game.

glossary.definition: An optimization technique used in the minimax algorithm to reduce the number of nodes that need to be evaluated by pruning branches that cannot influence the final decision.
glossary.sentence: We have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game.

glossary.definition: An approach that explores a limited number of levels denoted by n in a game tree to find the most promising moves using heuristic evaluation functions and minimax algorithm principles.
glossary.sentence: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: An approach that explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner by going one level deeper at each iteration until the time limit is reached or sufficient depth is achieved.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game.

glossary.definition: An entity in a game that aims to maximize its result or score.
glossary.sentence: the maximiser is to maximise his or her result in the game

glossary.definition: An entity in a game that aims to minimize its opponent's result or score.
glossary.sentence: the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game

glossary.definition: Assigned to nodes at MAX levels in Alpha-beta pruning. This value should not decrease as it represents a bad move and defines the best move that can be made to maximize game result.
glossary.sentence: Two numbers are used in alpha-beta pruning (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009): An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move.

glossary.definition: Assigned to nodes at MIN levels in Alpha-beta pruning. It should not increase as it represents a bad move and defines the best move that can be done to minimize game result.
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels. It should not increase, since this would represent a bad move. This number therefore defines the best move that can be done to minimise the result of the game.

glossary.definition: The process where unconsidered paths below a node are cut off when the α value of that node is found to be less than or equal to the β value of its ancestor.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node. The algorithm cuts off one unconsidered path.

glossary.definition: The process where unconsidered paths below a node are cut off when the β value of that node is found to be less than or equal to the α value of its ancestor.
glossary.sentence: the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A level in a game tree where nodes represent states favorable to the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MAX level, and thus acquires the maximum heuristic measure from its direct descendants, i.e. a value of 1

glossary.definition: A level in a game tree where nodes represent states favorable to the minimiser.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level and the only known heuristic measure is 1

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned to a node, representing its evaluation from the maximizer's perspective.
glossary.sentence: The α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned to a node, representing its evaluation from the minimizer's perspective.
glossary.sentence: the current node acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the level of the current node and the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants. The descendants of the current node have heuristic measures of −1 and −1. The current node is located at the MAX level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants, and the current node therefore acquires a heuristic measure of −1.

glossary.definition: A definitive evaluation assigned to a node based on its descendants' evaluations and its level in the game tree.
glossary.sentence: When the precise heuristic measure has been assigned, the algorithm goes up one level, via the same path that was used to go down. It finds that this node does not have other unconsidered descendants, and therefore assigns a precise heuristic measure based on the level of the current node and the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants.

glossary.definition: A tentative evaluation assigned to a node when it's not possible to determine a precise value due to the nature of the game or the position in the tree.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game. Step 17: When this precise heuristic measure has been assigned, the algorithm goes up one level, via the same path that was used to go down. It finds that this node one has another unconsidered descendant. It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: Paths in the game tree that are not currently being evaluated by the algorithm due to pruning processes (α-cut-off or β-cut-off).
glossary.sentence: After making the cut off, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far. Step 15: After making the cut off, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: The study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and uses for processing information.
glossary.sentence: Computer science is the broadest concept in this context.

glossary.definition: The mathematical principles that enable machine learning algorithms to operate on numerically presented and organized data in space.
glossary.sentence: Mathematics underlying machine learning allows it to operate on data that are numerically presented and organised in space.

glossary.definition: A branch of mathematics dealing with the collection, analysis, interpretation, presentation, and organization of data or real-world experiments.
glossary.sentence: Those working in this field need to be aware of statistical methods

glossary.definition: The branch of mathematics concerning the analysis of random phenomena. It provides a mathematical framework for describing and quantifying uncertainty.
glossary.sentence: probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques

glossary.definition: A subfield of mathematics concerning linear equations such as linear maps between vector spaces, and their representations in vector and matrix forms.
glossary.sentence: probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques

glossary.definition: The extension of calculus in one or more variables to functions of many variables.
glossary.sentence: probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques

glossary.definition: Methods for finding the best solution from all feasible solutions to a problem.
glossary.sentence: probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimisation techniques

glossary.definition: The process of designing, writing, testing, debugging, and maintaining the source code of computer programs.
glossary.sentence: knowledge in programming, algorithm theory, hardware and software engineering is needed to build a machine learning-based software

glossary.definition: The study of algorithms, their properties, and the classification of computational problems they can solve.
glossary.sentence: knowledge in programming, algorithm theory, hardware and software engineering is needed to build a machine learning-based software

glossary.definition: A particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values.
glossary.sentence: A data object (also called a data point, an observation, an example) - a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values

glossary.definition: A collection of data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: Attributes or characteristics used to describe data objects.
glossary.sentence: values of all features in the dataset are continuous. In that case, the Euclidean distance can be the right choice for measuring the similarity of data objects (Jones, 2009; Kubat, 2017)

glossary.definition: A specific value of a feature.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: A set of all features characterising a data object.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A configuration variable that is set before the learning process begins and remains constant during the learning process. In kNN algorithm, 'k' represents the chosen number of neighbours to examine.
glossary.sentence: Before you start using the algorithm, you need to define the value of the hyperparameter k , which represents the chosen number of neighbours that will be examined.

glossary.definition: A mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm. Certain configuration variables describe it (see 'Parameter' below).
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm

glossary.definition: A result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects

glossary.definition: An internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.
glossary.sentence: A parameter (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training

glossary.definition: A subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model

glossary.definition: The process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data

glossary.definition: A subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model

glossary.definition: Algorithms suitable for problems requiring hand-tuning, long lists of rules, or no good traditional solution; they simplify code and perform better in complex environments with fluctuating data.
glossary.sentence: In general, algorithms of machine learning are best suitable for (Geron, 2017): Problems for which existing solutions require a lot of hand-tuning or long lists of rules. Therefore, the development of conventional algorithms is time- and resource-consuming. One machine learning algorithm can often simplify code and perform better.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning where the model is trained using labeled data.
glossary.sentence: It is known that supervised machine learning is used for a regression and a classification task.

glossary.definition: A task of supervised learning where labels of data objects are presented as categories like spam or not spam, cat or dog, etc. The machine learning algorithm finds a category/class to which a new data object can be attributed.
glossary.sentence: Classification is performed when labels of data objects are presented as categories like spam or not spam, cat or dog, etc. Therefore, each data object belongs to a specific category/class. The machine learning algorithm finds a category/class to which a new data object can be attributed.

glossary.definition: A task of supervised learning where labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) like the price of a house or the number of graduating students. Therefore, for a new data object, the machine learning algorithm finds a prediction.
glossary.sentence: Regression is carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc. Therefore, for a new data object, the machine learning algorithm finds a prediction, for example, the prediction of the possible price of a house, the prediction of the potential number of graduating students, etc.

glossary.definition: A type of implicit learning based on unlabelled data. Its goal is to reveal patterns/structure of data and provides descriptive models. It's also used for data visualization.
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning is also called learning without a teacher. This type of learning is based on unlabelled data (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019). We can compare it with a child that has a set of bricks of different colours in front of him. Looking at them, he/she starts dividing them into different groups based on their colours.

glossary.definition: A task of unsupervised learning that deals with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters. Data objects belonging to a specific cluster share some common characteristics while characteristics differ between clusters.
glossary.sentence: Clustering deals with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters (Jones, 2008; Pant, 2019). Therefore, data objects belonging to a specific cluster share some common characteristics, while characteristics differ between clusters. Usually, the grouping of data objects is based on the distance between points or their similarity metrics.

glossary.definition: A reward-based learning suitable for agents operating in a changing environment who need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal. The agent learns how to act in the environment, receiving feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as 'My life-my rules!'. Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game- playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: type of learning where an agent learns without intervention from human, deciding which actions before the reinforcement were most responsible for it
glossary.sentence: The agent learns without intervention from human, for example, the two points for a win at the end of a chess game tells the agent it did something right and, thus, it is up to the agent to decide which of the actions before the reinforcement were most responsible for it (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: DeepMind's program that learned its optimal gaming policy by analysing millions of games and then playing many games against itself
glossary.sentence: For example, DeepMind's AlphaGo program learned its optimal gaming policy by analysing millions of games and then playing many games against itself (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: data processing in a supervised machine learning algorithm that leads to a specific output: a class label in a classification task or prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object in a regression task
glossary.sentence: A supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components : a decision process: the data processing that leads to a specific output.

glossary.definition: measures how good the output is by comparing it to known examples in a supervised machine learning algorithm
glossary.sentence: an error function: measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples.

glossary.definition: reduces the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm in a supervised machine learning algorithm
glossary.sentence: an updating or optimisation process: reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A machine learning technique that aims to find the best fit line for data objects by minimizing the total error across the whole dataset.
glossary.sentence: Linear Regression's task is to find the best possible values for weights that would provide the best fit line for the data objects (Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: feature values of data objects in Linear Regression algorithm
glossary.sentence: Input variables are feature values of data objects.

glossary.definition: prediction made by the linear regression model, a real number in a range from minus infinity to infinity in Linear Regression algorithm
glossary.sentence: The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model. It is a real number in a range from minus infinity to infinity.

glossary.definition: A type of problem in which data objects are assigned to specific classes or categories, predicted by a function that outputs discrete values.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression, in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task.

glossary.definition: A type of problem in which the output variable is a real value, predicted by a linear function based on input features.
glossary.sentence: Linear regression is suitable for the regression task.

glossary.definition: A mathematical relationship between two variables (x and y) represented by the equation y=m*x +b, where m is the slope and b is the intercept.
glossary.sentence: In mathematics, the linear relationship between two variables x and y is represented by the following function: y=m*x +b, where x – the value of the input variable, y – the value of the output variable, m – a real number that corresponds to the slope of the line, b – a real number that corresponds to the intercept (a point where the line crosses the y axis or, in other words, it is the value of y when x is equal to 0).

glossary.definition: A representation of a linear relationship as a straight line in two-dimensional space.
glossary.sentence: The equation given above describes a linear function represented as a straight line in the two-dimensional space (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: The difference between the right output and the predicted output in an artificial neural network.
glossary.sentence: Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression where only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used.
glossary.sentence: There are two types of linear regression (Russell and Norvig, 2010): univariate (or simple) linear regression – when only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression where each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model is used.
glossary.sentence: multivariate (multiple) linear regression – when each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model As the task of the linear regression is to find the line or plane (depending on the type of the linear regression) that best fits the data given, then training a linear regression model means finding the values of the weights that minimise the empirical loss (error) across all data objects available in the dataset (Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A measure used to evaluate the performance of a regression algorithm; it accumulates differences between predicted and actual output values.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: An optimization technique used in linear regression to minimize the cost function by iteratively updating the weights of the linear function based on partial derivatives.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent. The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values (Google Developers, n.d.; Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. It determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.
glossary.sentence: A learning rate is one of the hyperparameters used in artificial neural networks. It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.

glossary.definition: The point where the algorithm minimizes the error in its predictions.
glossary.sentence: So, the process of building a mathematical model will be slow and computationally intensive. But, on the other hand, if the learning rate is too high, the algorithm can miss the error minima.

glossary.definition: A type of classification task where there are only two classes/categories.
glossary.sentence: The structure of the confusion matrix differs a little bit for the binary classification task and multiclass classification. Table 1 represents the structure of the confusion matrix for the binary classification, while Table 2 gives an example of the matrix.

glossary.definition: A classification algorithm that uses a linear function followed by a sigmoid function to predict discrete class labels, typically 0 or 1.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression also receives as input data the values of the features of data objects, but outputs a binary class label of this object as a result.

glossary.definition: A mathematical function used in logistic regression to transform linear output into a probability value between 0 and 1.
glossary.sentence: After receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function. The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.

glossary.definition: A simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks by finding the most similar neighbours in the dataset for new data objects.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: The basis for algorithms, representing how close data objects are based on feature-based descriptions (feature similarity). The notion of similarity varies depending on the data type.
glossary.sentence: In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity) (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A measure of the geometric distance between two points in space. In the context of data analysis, it is used to calculate how dissimilar two instances are among continuous variables.
glossary.sentence: For each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset.

glossary.definition: A measure of the distance between two points in a grid-based system. In the context of data analysis, it is used to calculate how dissimilar two instances are among categorical variables.
glossary.sentence: For each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset.

glossary.definition: A measure of the minimum number of substitutions required to change one string into another. In the context of data analysis, it is used to calculate how dissimilar two instances are among categorical variables.
glossary.sentence: For each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset.

glossary.definition: Datasets containing both continuous and categorical feature values.
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017)

glossary.definition: A simple machine learning algorithm that does not have many hyperparameters to tune and does not assume anything about data distribution. It uses all data in the dataset for making predictions by calculating distances between a new data object and existing ones, based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance. The output is determined by the majority label (classification) or average value (regression) of 'k' nearest neighbors.
glossary.sentence: The kNN algorithm is simple enough and does not have many hyperparameters that should be tuned. Moreover, from the steps given above, one can conclude that this algorithm does not have a separate training phase. It uses all data contained in the dataset for making predictions.

glossary.definition: A version of kNN where a new data object is classified based on the single nearest neighbor.
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: An unsupervised machine learning algorithm used for clustering. It divides an available dataset into subsets (clusters) based on similarity of data objects within a cluster and difference between clusters. The algorithm minimises the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid, which is the average of feature vectors within the cluster.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), “the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement” .

glossary.definition: The centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance. It represents the average of all feature vectors within the cluster.
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A subset of data objects in a dataset that share some similarities. Each cluster is characterised by similarity of data objects within the cluster and difference between clusters.
