glossary.sentence: Therefore, each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterised by two aspects: the similarity of data objects within the cluster; the difference of data objects between the clusters.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that does not require specifying the number of clusters before operation; instead, developers decide where to cut the dendrogram for the final number of clusters and which method of linkage to use.
glossary.sentence: This algorithm does not demand to specify the number of clusters before the algorithm operation. Still, the developer needs to decide a) where to cut the dendrogram to receive the final number of clusters and b) which method of linkage to use to measure similarity between clusters.

glossary.definition: A bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters where each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster.
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters. Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach that starts with a cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters at each hierarchy level.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters. The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity.

glossary.definition: Methods used to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters in hierarchical clustering, often called linkage methods. These include Complete-linkage, Single-linkage, and Average-linkage.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods . The most used methods are (Hastie, 2017) : Complete-linkage ; Single-linkage; Average-linkage.

glossary.definition: An ordered sequence of cluster merges in hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Summarising the information about the hierarchical clustering given in this topic, it is worth mentioning that the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges (Hastie, 2017).

glossary.definition: A line in hierarchical clustering that intersects vertical lines, resulting in clusters with specific data objects.
glossary.sentence: For example, in Figure 6, Cut-off 1 intersects two vertical lines, and we receive two clusters with the following data objects: (A, B, C, D) and (E). Cut-off 2 intersects 3 vertical lines, so we receive three clusters with the following data objects: (A, B), (C, D) and (E).

glossary.definition: A metric used to measure prediction errors in regression tasks. It is calculated as the average of squared differences between predicted and actual values.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for regression tasks In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range. Thus, its output is a real value. Since the regression is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted labels. The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.

glossary.definition: A metric used to measure prediction errors in regression tasks, which provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable by applying a square root to MSE.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”. The smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is.

glossary.definition: A metric used to measure prediction errors in regression tasks. It provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable by averaging absolute differences between predicted and actual values.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: A coefficient that indicates how close a model fits the actual data objects available in the dataset, ranging from 0 (no fit) to 1 (perfect fit).
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset. The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit.

glossary.definition: Correct predictions in binary classification where a positive data object is correctly recognized as such.
glossary.sentence: Predictions made in the classification task can lead to four types of outcomes (Kubat, 2017) : Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: Correct predictions in binary classification where a negative data object is correctly recognized as such.
glossary.sentence: Predictions made in the classification task can lead to four types of outcomes (Kubat, 2017) : Correct predictions: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: Erroneous predictions in binary classification where a negative data object is labeled as positive.
glossary.sentence: Predictions made in the classification task can lead to four types of outcomes (Kubat, 2017) : Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).

glossary.definition: Erroneous predictions in binary classification where a positive data object is labeled as negative.
glossary.sentence: Predictions made in the classification task can lead to four types of outcomes (Kubat, 2017) : Erroneous predictions: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).

glossary.definition: A type of classification task where there are three or more classes/categories.
glossary.sentence: In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category).

glossary.definition: A two-dimensional table that summarizes the performance of a classification model.
glossary.sentence: It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010). Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.

glossary.definition: A correctly predicted positive instance in a binary classification task or a specific class in multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: An incorrectly predicted positive instance in binary classification or misclassification of data objects to other classes in multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: Taking a specific class/category, we can consider the values at the intersections of the column of the corresponding class with other classes as false positives, while values at the intersections of the row of the corresponding class with other classes as false negatives.

glossary.definition: A correctly predicted negative instance in binary classification or correct predictions for data objects not belonging to a specific class in multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: Other cells that do not intersect the column and row corresponding to a specific class/category represent true negatives for this specific class.

glossary.definition: An incorrectly predicted negative instance in binary classification or misclassification of data objects to other classes in multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: Taking a specific class/category, we can consider the values at the intersections of the column of the corresponding class with other classes as false positives, while values at the intersections of the row of the corresponding class with other classes as false negatives.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value of accuracy lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.

glossary.definition: The ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to the total predicted positives.
glossary.sentence: In the context of binary classification tasks, precision is calculated as TP / (TP + FP), where TP is true positives and FP are false positives.

glossary.definition: The ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to all observations in the actual class. It reflects the classifier's ability to find all relevant cases within a dataset.
glossary.sentence: In binary classification tasks, recall is calculated as TP / (TP + FN), where TP is true positives and FN are false negatives.

glossary.definition: The average of precisions for all classes in multiclass classification tasks. Calculated by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, we first calculate a precision for each class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the column corresponding to the class as false positives. After that, we calculate the macro average precision for the task by summing up precisions for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.

glossary.definition: The average of recalls for all classes in multiclass classification tasks. Calculated by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives. After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A dataset where classes are not equally represented. It can lead to misleading evaluation metrics like accuracy.
glossary.sentence: However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: The inverse relationship between precision and recall. Increasing one usually reduces the other.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa.

glossary.definition: A simple, non-predictive model used for comparison purposes. It may achieve high accuracy but lacks predictive power.
glossary.sentence: In this case, a poorly built “dummy” model which always predicts Class 0, achieves a 95% Accuracy, which indicates a very strong model. However, this model is not really predictive and Accuracy is not the right performance metric to evaluate the power of this model

glossary.definition: A metric used in classification tasks that represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall, aiming to estimate the number of data objects classified correctly. It lies within the range [0,1]. A high F-score indicates good model performance when both recall and precision are high and close to each other.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A graphical representation that plots the false positive rate against the true positive rate for different classification thresholds. It helps visualize trade-offs between sensitivity and specificity, with an ideal classifier having a curve reaching the upper-left corner.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: Area under the ROC Curve. It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve and provides a single-number summary of classifier performance, ranging from 0 (random chance) to 1 (perfect classifier).
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010). It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5 (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A metric for clustering tasks that measures the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in neighboring clusters. Values range from -1 to 1, with closer values to +1 indicating better-separated clusters.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Score is calculated by averaging intra-cluster distance (a) and inter-cluster distance (b), representing the closeness of each data object in a cluster to neighboring clusters. The values of the Silhouette score lies in a range [-1, 1].

glossary.definition: A computational model consisting of artificial neurons connected in a pattern to process information.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern...

glossary.definition: A cell in the human brain responsible for processing and transmitting information through electrical and chemical signals.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon.

glossary.definition: The process by which a neuron generates an electrical signal (action potential) in response to stimulation.
glossary.sentence: The single most important fact about the firing of neurons is that it depends upon the activity at the synapses.

glossary.definition: A structure that permits a neuron to pass an electrochemical signal to another neuron or target cell.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: A type of synapse that increases the likelihood a neuron will fire an action potential.
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight...

glossary.definition: A type of synapse that decreases the likelihood a neuron will fire an action potential.
glossary.sentence: ...and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight...

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of a biological neuron that processes inputs and generates outputs based on specific functions.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron...

glossary.definition: Numerical values that enter an artificial neuron from the environment or other neurons.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neuron has one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals)...

glossary.definition: Real-valued parameters in an artificial neuron that express the strength of each input.
glossary.sentence: The numerical values from the environment typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset. The inputs typically are labelled by indexed x; a set of real-valued weights (one for each input)...

glossary.definition: A numerical value produced by an artificial neuron that can be either a final solution or input to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: ...an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.

glossary.definition: A mathematical operation used in artificial neural networks to combine input signals.
glossary.sentence: a function for combining input signals (for example, the summation function )

glossary.definition: A function that is applied inside each processing unit after calculating the weighted sum, including bias input.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: An additional input in an artificial neuron that provides offset from the origin.
glossary.sentence: A bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin.

glossary.definition: A layer of neurons in an artificial neural network that receives inputs from the environment.
glossary.sentence: The neurons connected to the external environment form input and output layers.

glossary.definition: A layer in a neural network consisting of processing units, which perform feature detection. Neurons in this layer cannot be observed through input/output behavior, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output should be.
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons; at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: Individual components within an artificial neural network that perform computations and apply activation functions.
glossary.sentence: The output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.

glossary.definition: A method used to modify weights in an artificial neural network, allowing it to adapt and improve its performance.
glossary.sentence: a learning rule to adapt the weights

glossary.definition: An artificial neural network with connections only in one direction. Processing units receive input from upstream and deliver output to downstream processing units, with no loops.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from 'upstream' processing units and delivers output to 'downstream' processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: An artificial neural network where outputs are fed back into inputs, forming a dynamic system that may exhibit various behaviors based on initial state and previous inputs.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: A type of processing unit in a neural network that processes input based on summation and activation functions. It adjusts its weights when the predicted output differs from the real output, according to the delta rule.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron; the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit; after receiving the output of the perceptron, the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output; if the predicted output is different from the real output, then the perceptron adjusts the weights

glossary.definition: The simplest and commonly known type of artificial neural network with practical applicability limited to linearly separable functions.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: A method used to compute weight corrections in artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule : Therefore, the complete rule for adjustments of the weights of the perceptron is the following one:

glossary.definition: One cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes. It corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes. Therefore, the number of epochs corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network, with one hidden layer capable of representing any continuous function of input signals. With two hidden layers, it can represent even discontinuous functions.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A network that processes information in one direction, from input to output, without looping back.
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons; an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: A condition that stops the learning process in artificial neural networks. It can be reaching a pre-defined maximum permissible error or achieving a pre-defined number of iterations.
glossary.sentence: The learning can be stopped if the total classification error on all training examples reaches the pre-defined maximum permissible error or the pre-defined number of iterations is achieved.

glossary.definition: A type of feedforward artificial neural network with multiple layers between input and output layers.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm .

glossary.definition: An algorithm used for training artificial neural networks by minimizing the difference between predicted and actual output.
glossary.sentence: It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation .

glossary.definition: The process of propagating inputs over the network from the input layer to the output layer during training.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels. It means that by feeding a particular data object into the network, we expect to acquire a known output. However, in reality, the output predicted by the network differs from the actual output.

glossary.definition: The neurons in the hidden layers of an artificial neural network that contribute to the total error at the output.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the weights of all layers contribute to the total error. Therefore, to reduce the error at the output, the responsibility for the error should be shared among all layers.

glossary.definition: The way of encoding knowledge within a system, represented naturally by 'if...then...' constructions.
glossary.sentence: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A collection of production rules that an expert system uses to solve problems in a specific domain.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base for real-world problems includes a considerable number of production rules. In each iteration, all production rules stored in the knowledge base are matched against the pattern

glossary.definition: A component of an expert system that relates production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions they support.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a task of the inference engine is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support

glossary.definition: A system that stores unrelated facts, like purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students. The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: A set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge in a computational system, which can be represented as logical statements, networked graphs, structured complex structures, or procedural instructions.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. They work closely with domain experts due to not being specialized in all problem domains.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains. This is a reason why he/she works in a close collaboration with the domain expert.

glossary.definition: An individual who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular, usually limited, domain. They possess domain-specific heuristic knowledge to solve problems effectively.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems within a narrowly specialized domain at the level and manner of human experts.
glossary.sentence: An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A category of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge in the form of logical statements using constants, variables, functions, predicates, and logical operators.
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.

glossary.definition: A category of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema where each node in the graph represents a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts. Relations are not represented explicitly by arcs but through a referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts. Moreover, relations are not represented explicitly by arcs at it was done in semantic networks, but a referencing principle is used when one node references another node by its name.

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: A category of knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge in the form of procedures or instructions.
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: A relation representing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts (Fig.6). A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A relation representing one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to (Fig.7). The direction of the relation is from an example to a class/set/type/kind.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to (Fig.7).

glossary.definition: A relation representing parts of the object to the whole object (Fig.8). Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance. The direction of the relation is from parts to whole objects.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8). It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object. Without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance.

glossary.definition: A relation representing objects/concepts to their properties (Fig.9). A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of” (Fig.9). It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties (Fig.9).

glossary.definition: A relation representing properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10). This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”. The direction of the relation is from the property to its value.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value” (Fig.10). It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10).

glossary.definition: Sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame. Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation schema where each node represents a single concept/object, and relations are represented explicitly by arcs. They represent declarative knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts.
glossary.sentence: Semantic networks have disadvantages: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts ); they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways; they do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge like negation, disjunction, or also all/part/several/nobody/many, etc.

glossary.definition: A data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept or information relevant to stereotyped entities. It has a specific structure including slot titles, slot values and facets.
glossary.sentence: Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas. Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975. They are based on the idea that when encountering a new situation or making changes to one's view of the present problem, one selects from memory a structure called a Frame.

glossary.definition: A slot can have a procedural attachment, which includes regular procedures and/or demons.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames that represents extended knowledge about slot values and tells the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. It helps represent a problem in an abstract form.
glossary.sentence: According to Minsky (1975), “collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems” . Thus, to create a knowledge base for the frame-based expert system, we need to define and interrelate frames. There are two main types of frames (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): A class/generic frame.

glossary.definition: Includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind.
glossary.sentence: Typically, a class frame includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: Classes derived from other classes.
glossary.sentence: Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes (Figure 5).

glossary.definition: Represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain.
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: A unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame.
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: Defined between TreeAlex-Person, BrownLucie-Person, Tom-Cat, Rex-Dog, Cat-Animal, and Dog-Animal.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal .

glossary.definition: Allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: The Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: Allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base, triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Triggered when a slot value is unspecified and added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame.

glossary.definition: Triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame.

glossary.definition: Acquired when demons are assigned to frames.
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames. There are standard demons defined for frames, although different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases can offer a slightly different set of demons with different titles.

glossary.definition: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for this class.
glossary.sentence: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame;

glossary.definition: Tom is an instance for this class.
glossary.sentence: Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame;

glossary.definition: Rex is an instance for this class.
glossary.sentence: Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame;

glossary.definition: Represents the hierarchical organization of knowledge.
glossary.sentence: It is represented as a Class slot in the sub-class frames and instance frames.

glossary.definition: BrownLucie - Tom, the relation Owns; Tom - BrownLucie, the relation Animal owner; TreeAlex - Rex, the relation Owns; Rex - TreeAlex, the relation Animal owner; Tom – Rex, the relation Afraid of.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner; TreeAlex - Rex , the relation Owns , and Rex- TreeAlex , the relation Animal owner; Tom – Rex , the relation Afraid of .

glossary.definition: Stores knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way, provides hierarchical organization of knowledge, allows representation of various knowledge about objects, supports inheritance.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance;

glossary.definition: A chunk of knowledge about solving a problem. It consists of IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion.
glossary.sentence: Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A rule-based expert system that consists of three parts: knowledge base, working memory, and inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A temporary storage used by an expert system to hold information about the current state of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: the working memory contains a pattern corresponding to the current state of the problem-solving process

glossary.definition: A pattern-directed search in the knowledge base that matches the content of the working memory against production rules' IF or THEN parts, depending on the search direction. It involves enabling a conflict set of matching rules, selecting one using a conflict resolution strategy, and updating the working memory with the selected rule's IF or THEN part.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: A search strategy in rule-based expert systems where the process begins with a problem description or given data about the problem, adding it to the working memory. The control cycle matches this pattern against the IF part of production rules, firing a rule and adding its THEN part to the working memory until the goal is found.
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory

glossary.definition: A search strategy in rule-based expert systems where an expert system has a goal, and the inference engine attempts to find evidence to prove it. The control cycle matches this pattern against the THEN part of production rules, firing a rule and adding its IF part to the working memory until facts about the problem are found.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory

glossary.definition: A method for choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, known as the conflict set. Strategies include firing the first/last rule found, based on priorities, or more specific rules.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A set of 'if...then...' conditions that represent knowledge in a rule-based expert system. They determine how to search the knowledge base and influence conflict resolution strategies.
glossary.sentence: This form of the rules is critical in controlling search, making the system behave logically. The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.term: Control through selection of search direction
glossary.definition: A strategy used to make the search for production rule matches more effective by choosing between data-driven (forward chaining) and goal-driven (backward chaining) searches.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction

glossary.term: Control through selection of rule sequence and structure
glossary.definition: A strategy used to make the search for production rule matches more effective by organizing rules based on their logical dependencies.
glossary.sentence: control through the selection of rule sequence and structure

glossary.definition: An individual who interacts with an expert system to provide known facts or goals for problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: demand the user to deliver known facts and then the system can switch on to the goal-driven search

glossary.definition: An operator in production rules that introduces ambiguity and should be avoided if possible.
glossary.sentence: try avoiding the use of OR in production rules because this operator introduces ambiguity; if it is not possible to avoid using OR, try avoiding the use of combinations of AND and OR in the same rule

glossary.definition: A logical operator used in production rules that combines conditions.
glossary.sentence: IF a AND b THEN c more specific rule

glossary.definition: The conclusion or action part of the 'if...then...' structure in a production rule.
glossary.sentence: try adding only one action or statement/conclusion to the THEN part of production rules

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which a choice must be made according to a conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: the conflict set is not assembled at all, and the order in which rules are selected for examination determines conflict resolution

glossary.definition: An explicitly stated value assigned to a rule that influences its selection during conflict resolution based on priorities.
glossary.sentence: Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen

glossary.definition: A method for conflict resolution where a specific rule processes more information than a general one.
glossary.sentence: to fire rules based on their priorities. to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one

glossary.definition: Values attached to each fact in working memory, allowing for firing rules based on the recency of data entry.
glossary.sentence: A rule is more specific than another if it has more conditions: IF a THEN c IF a AND b THEN c more specific rule fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory

glossary.definition: The structure offered by production rules that allows each rule to be an independent unit of knowledge, facilitating self-documentation.
glossary.sentence: Uniform structure. Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented

glossary.definition: The division between where knowledge resides (knowledge base) and how it is processed (inference engine), allowing for independent modification.
glossary.sentence: Separation of knowledge and control. Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine. The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules

glossary.definition: The syntactic independence of rules that enables incremental development by adding, deleting or modifying knowledge without affecting other rules directly.
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules

glossary.definition: The ability to display selected rules during execution and provide meaningful explanations due to each rule's single 'chunk' of problem-solving knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Tracing and explanation. The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules. At each stage of the control cycle "Recognize-act", the selected rule can be displayed. In addition, because each rule corresponds to a single “chunk” of problem-solving knowledge, the rule content can provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action

glossary.definition: Values added to production rules that allow for representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors

glossary.definition: Applications requiring immediate responses, where the computational resource consumption of inference mechanisms can be a limiting factor.
glossary.sentence: Expert systems with a large set of rules (over 100 rules) can be slow, and thus can be unsuitable for real-time applications

glossary.definition: The ability to adapt and update the knowledge base based on new information or outcomes.
glossary.sentence: rule-based expert systems are not able to learn from the experience and understand which rules are already out-of-date
